[description action="start"] Ratibida columnifera . Plant this perennial wildflower in your organic flower garden, or grow it in containers. Ratibida Prairie Coneflower seeds are useful for water-wise xeriscaping plans because the plants are native to the central prairies and mountains, and accustomed to drought, high heat, and high humidity. The central elongated cone of each flower is surrounded by drooping bright yellow petals. The strap-like leaves are bluish green and form a rosette. Avoid getting the leaves wet (or water only first thing in the morning) to prevent the onset of foliar disorders like powdery mildew. Flowers appear from late spring to autumn, and stems can range from 60-150cm (24-60") tall. Perennial. This species of Echinacea is similar to Echinacea pallida (Pale Purple Cone Flower) with shorter broader leaves, and blooms about a month later. A well known medicinal herb, Echinacea is a cold hardy perennial plant that usually grows with single purple flower The flowers and roots are commonly used to make tea Native to parts of eastern North America, grows wild in Ontario and the eastern US. Grows up to 47" / 120cm tall. Blooms through summer into fall. Flowers are usually but not always purple. Flowers are 3-6" / 7-15cm. Germinates best with fluctuating daily temperatures. Package Contains: 40+ Seeds Hardiness Zone: 3 Stratification: None required Growing: Direct sow in fall or early spring 1/8” deep, mulch.

A fast growing annual herb growing 2-3 ft tall and it's in the mint family. Monarda citriodora, commonly known as Lemon Bee Balm or Lemon Mint, is a native wildflower in North America. The plant gets its name from its lemony fragrance, which makes it a delightful addition to gardens and landscapes. Lemon Bee Balm produces clusters of lavender-pink flowers that attract pollinators like bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. It is a compact and bushy plant, reaching a height of 1 to 3 feet, with aromatic leaves and square stems. Gardeners appreciate Lemon Bee Balm for its ornamental and culinary uses. The leaves can be used to make herbal teas and add a lemony flavor to dishes. This perennial plant is relatively low-maintenance and can thrive in full sun to partial shade. Lemon Bee Balm is also known for its medicinal properties and is used in traditional herbal medicine for its soothing qualities. It's an excellent choice for pollinator gardens, herb gardens, and native plant landscapes. Consider planting Lemon Bee Balm near tomatoes and other vegetables to deter insect pests. Seeds can be sown directly in the garden in the spring or fall, and the plant will readily self-sow for future growth. Growing Characteristics: Annual Stratification: none required

[description action="start"] Festuca arundinacea . Turf type. Tall Fescue stands up to traffic, drought, cold, shade, pets, and mowing. It is far tougher than conventional lawn grasses like Kentucky blue grass, but it still makes a lush green lawn. It is commonly used by municipalities for planting recreation fields and school grounds. This fescue is a tough perennial. Officially, it is hardy to Zone 4, but a sudden onset of very cold weather can interfere with its dormancy, causing winterkill. Sow in the spring or fall (fall is recommended) at a depth of 5-10mm (¼-½”). Because this product does not produce thatch, it is not attractive to the chafer beetle. Fescue can be mixed with wildflower blends or Micro-Clover to cover in an area densely and quickly. It still requires mowing approximately every two weeks in the growing season. Use Tall Fescue to overseed existing lawn or seed a new area, with no turf present. Seeding rates are included on packaging. Sow Rate: 3.6kg (8 lbs) per 1,000 square feet Fescue is also a major component of our Chafer Beetle Resistant Lawn Blend . [description action="end"][quickfacts action="start quickfacts"] Drought resistant Grows in part shade Stands up to pets Hardy to Zone 4 Tougher than grass [quickfacts action="end quickfacts"]

Wild raspberry seeds from native stands collected from the forest around our tree nursery. They spread and cover the understory, fully cold hardy to zone -40C they grow prolifically even in the shade. With more sun exposure you'll get more berries. They grow the same sized fruit as cultivar raspberries, but more spread out. Plants usually grow to 4-5' tall. The leaves are great for herbal tea. Package Contains: 150+ Seeds Hardiness Zone: 2-3 Stratification Required: 2-3 months Storage: Keep seeds cool and dry until planting Planting instructions: For best results fall plant into prepared seed beds. Seeds can also be cold stratified in a refrigerator and plant into seedbeds or pots in the spring. Sow 1/4" deep and cover with mulch.
Wild Raspberry Seeds (Rubus idaeus) - Native Perennial  - Zone 3 - 150+ Seeds

Wild Sarsaparilla is a member of the Ginseng family, it's a perennial herb that grows well in the shade and covers the the walking trails in the forest up to about knee high. The leaves are edible, and quite tasty when they're small in the spring. It has a distinctive stalk that that branches into three stems with compound leaves. The plant has a history of medicinal uses, and the roots are also edible. Package Contains: 50+ Seeds Hardiness Zone: 3 Stratification Required: 3-5 months of cold stratification. For best results plant outdoors in the fall and let nature do the rest. Storage: Store in a cool dry place Planting instructions: Plant outdoors in fall, or cold stratify in the fridge over the winter 3-5 months. Sow 1/4 - 1/2" deep and cover with mulch.
Wild Sarsaparilla Seeds (Aralia nudicaulis) - Wild Native Perennial Plant Seeds - Hardiness Zone 3 - 50+ Seeds

We have fresh Chokecherry ( Prunus virginiana ) seeds available. These are collected from local wild stands of healthy trees with the largest cherries. Chokecherry are a prolific species in the Prunus genus, which makes them related to sweet cherries but also plums and other stone fruits. The cherries are 3/8 - 7/16" diameter and turn black when ripe. These small trees / bushes are fully cold hardy in our area and grow well in sandy soil, they thrive here. They grow well in the forest with companion Saskatoons and Hazelnuts, but will grow largest and produce the most fruit when grown in full sun with plenty of room to spread out. Our stands of Chokecherry bear large fruit, and despite the name we find they are tasty and sweet enough to eat fresh off the tree. An enjoyable trail side snack when fully ripe. Popular for making jelly and wine. Excellent for wildlife. Fruit ripens in August and hangs on the tree through September into late fall. Hardiness Zone: 2-3 Stratification Required: 3-4 months Germination Testing: Cut test Storage: Keeping refrigerated in a sealed container at 3-5C. Seeds can be viable for up to several years. Planting instructions: Fall plant into prepared seed beds. Optionally soak in water for 24 hrs before planting. Sow 1/4 - 1/2" deep and cover with mulch. Choke Cherry - Natural Resources Canada
Chokecherry Seeds (Prunus virginiana var. melanocarpa) Black-Fruited Variety - 100+ Seeds

Seeds for a wild and potent variety of tobacco plant native to North America also known as Aztec tobacco. Used by Indigenous people through history in ceremonies. Has a history of cultivation and use in Asia and Russia and parts of Europe. Nicotine also also a natural pesticide. Annual plant that grows 5 ft (1.5m) tall. Package Contains: 250+ Seeds Stratification: None required Planting instructions: Plant in the spring or start indoors and transplant after last frost.
Wild Tobacco Seeds (Nicotiana rustica, Aztec Tobacco) - Annual - 250+ Seeds

An aromatic silver-green plant that grows wild around our forest in clearings and meadows. We collected the wild seed this locally (Zone 3). Grows 12-36" (30-90cm) tall. It's perennial and regrows from the ground every year, it's attractive and a food source for wildlife. It's also the plant used for smudging, where the plant is tied into small bundles and burned like incense. Hardiness Zone: 3 Cold Stratification: 1 month

Cattails are a reedy marsh plant with distinct velvety brown cigar shaped spikes. Important for wildlife, they provide food and shelter for birds, mammals and fish. The plants are edible, including the starchy roots, the new shoots and the pollen can be even used as a flour. Start new plants from seed and they will reproduce vegetatively through rhizomes. Our seed were collected locally in Southern Manitoba. Grows up to 10 ft (3m) tall Quantity: 1000+ Seeds (they're very small with bits of fluff attached) Stratification: none required Cold hardiness: Zone 3 Germination: Plant 1/4 deep in pots and provide some bottom heat or keep warm 100F/37C for up to two weeks. Transplant out at 1-2 months.
Cattail Seeds (Typha angustifolia, Narrow-leaved Cattail) - Perennial - Zone 3 - 1000+ Seeds

Red Elderberry is a fast growing highly branching native shrub with attractive leaves and bright red berries. The fruit is edible by wildlife. We collected these seeds locally in Zone 3. White spruce seed ( Picea glauca ) collected locally in the fall. The seed is from a large healthy tree, 60' tall with a 30" diameter trunk. White spruce can live to be 250-350 years old. White spruce are a hardy tree that can handle some shade and they're prolific in northern forests. We find they transplant easier than pines. When you start them for seed, like all conifers - it takes some patience but worth it. Plan to keep them in a seed bed for the first two years where you can water them and provide some shade while the get established. After the second growing season they're still small (you're doing great if they're 6") and will be large enough to plant out. Another option is to grow them in plug trays, which works if you keep them consistently watered and have a very insulated location for them for the winter. Young trees while they establish will grow about six inches/year until they reach about 10 years old then they start to take off and can grow 12-18" pear year. When you transplant them it's good give them a sheltered site and some mulch. Hardiness Zone: 2 Stratification Required: 30-45 days Seed type: Winged (you can dewing them by rubbing, but they're fine to plant with the little wings) Storage: Keep stored seeds cool and dry. Planting instructions: Plant outdoors in seedbeds in the fall, or stratify in your fridge and plant in early spring. Plant 1/4" deep and lightly mulch.

It features striking daisy-like flowers with prominent, cone-shaped centers surrounded by drooping purple petals. Narrow-Leaved Coneflower typically grows to a height of 1 to 3 feet (30-90 cm). Known for its drought tolerance and ability to thrive in various soil types. It's a valuable plant for attracting pollinators like bees and butterflies to your garden. Gardeners often cultivate it for its aesthetic appeal and potential health benefits. Prefers full sun to partial shade and well-drained soil. Hardy and resilient, making it suitable for different garden settings. Seeds can be directly sown in the garden or started indoors for transplanting. A versatile addition to both ornamental and medicinal gardens. Hardiness Zone: 3 Cold Stratify: 2-3 months

The Siberian Stone Pine is a slow-growing evergreen tree native to the cold climates of Siberia and northeastern Asia. It typically reaches 10–20 meters at maturity, with a dense, conical crown. Its needles are soft, dark green, and grow in bundles of five, giving it a lush appearance. This tree produces edible seeds, often referred to as pine nuts, encased in woody cones that mature after 2–3 years. Adapted to harsh environments, it is tolerant of extreme cold, thriving in poor, acidic soils. It is highly valued for reforestation and windbreaks in northern climates and can live for centuries in suitable conditions. Cold Hardiness: Zone 1 Seed Source: Manitoba Cold Stratification: 2 months warm followed by 3-4 months cold.

Jack Pine seed ( Pinus banksiana ) collected locally in the fall, near our nursery in Southern Manitoba (Zone 3). The seed is from a stand of large healthy trees. Jack pine cones are serotinous, which means in nature they open in the heat of a forest fire. The cones can stay closed on the tree hugging the small branches for years waiting to open. They're very cold hardy and common in northern forests, once established they grow best in full sun. We've grown them for years and find them easy to germinate, they're kind of tough. The small trees like other conifers take some patience, at the end of the first growing season they're just a few inches tall. Grow them for two seasons before you plant them out into a permanent location, seedbeds will give you the best winter protection and are the lowest maintenance method. You can plant them close, up to a max of 50 seedlings per square foot. Plug trays are another good option which will mean you're plant out plugs vs bare root seedlings, just make sure to insulate and water them well over the winter. We used to use plug trays and moved to seed beds because they're easier. Hardiness Zone: 2 Stratification Required: none required, but if you stratify them in the fridge for a couple of weeks you can get more consistent germination. Seed type: Winged (you can dewing them by rubbing, but they're fine to plant with the little wings) Storage: Keep stored seeds cool and dry. Planting instructions: Plant outdoors in seedbeds in the fall, or plant in early spring. Plant 1/4" deep and lightly mulch.

Canada Wild Plum is a hardy native tree from the prairies and parklands of Manitoba, and it's relatively rare. The fruit is red and yellow and ripens here in late august usually after American wild plum has dropped, typically 1" - 1.25" with an oblong shape, the plums are sweet with a tart skin. The trees have a heavy set of white flowers in the spring and are widely considered the best pollinator for other hybrid plum trees, and they're valuable for wildlife. They can be used for rootstocks and will usually grow with more of a single trunk tree form, they don't sucker as much as American wild plum. These seeds were collected from an old orchard rows, grown under open pollination with just the other wild plums in the row. They were probably planted for rootstocks 30-40 years ago. The trees show the broad, rounded leaves and scalloped (crenate-serrate) margins typical of Prunus nigra, the fruit is very typical yellow/red color and form, larger and later ripening than American wild plum. Open pollinated seed means there is natural variation and there could be some percentage of hybridization, but given the isolated location, seedlings should be true to type. Seed Source: Manitoba Cold hardiness: Zone 2–3 Height: 3–5 m (small tree / large shrub) Uses: fresh eating, preserves, rootstock, shelterbelts, wildlife plantings Pollination: requires cross-pollination with another plum for best fruit set Stratification Required: Warm stratify 1-2 months followed by 3-4 months of cold stratification

Blue Spruce, scientifically known as Picea pungens, is a majestic evergreen conifer native to the Rocky Mountains of the United States. Renowned for its striking blue foliage and symmetrical form, this tree typically reaches heights of 50 to 75 feet, sometimes growing as tall as 150 feet. The tree has a slow to moderate growth rate and a lifespan of 150-600 years. Blue Spruce has bluish-green pointed needles about 1 inch long and a trunk diameter of around 5 feet. It produces pale brown cones approximately 4 inches long. This tree thrives in humid canyon bottoms, steep mountain ranges, and mid-montane forests at high elevations. It prefers cool and humid climatic zones where the annual precipitation mainly occurs in the summer. The Blue Spruce is hardy in USDA zones 3-7, tolerating winter temperatures as low as -40°F (-40°C). However, it struggles in regions with hot summers and requires adequate moisture and shade to prevent heat stress. Cold Hardiness Zone: 3 Seed Source: CO, USA (Zone 4 - 5) Stratification: Blue Spruce seeds require about 30 days of cold, moist stratification at temperatures of 3°C (37°F) to 5°C (41°F). Planting Instructions: Soak seeds in water for 24 hours, then place in a bag with moist sand or vermiculite. Refrigerate for 30 days before sowing 1/8 inch deep in well-draining soil. Keep soil moist but not wet, and provide full sun for optimal growth.
